rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Hrg On Petition For Relief
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 15, 2014, Line 2: PETITIONER GARY LAWMAN'S Notice of Hearing On Petition For Relief. Petition granted. Petitioner produced sufficient evidence to establish his condition of mental incapacity for the duration of the claim-filing period, and that his mental incapacity was the cause of his failure to file a claim. Petitioner's evidence established that Dr. Katz was qualified to render his opinion which was well supported by examination, interviews, physical examination and review of the medical records. CCSF misunderstands the requirements of Gov't Code sec. 946.6, which provides that petitioner must show failure to timely present the claim was due either to excusable neglect (see Gov't Code sec. 946.6(c)(1)) or to petitioner's mental incapacity (see Gov't Code sec. 946.6(c)(3)). Here, petitioner relies on the latter provision rendering CCSF's excusable neglect arguments irrelevant. Additionally, CCSF failed to produce any evidence to contradict petitioner's evidence. The court finds that the petition is supported by a preponderance of the evidence. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: HARGROVE, MICHAEL C. DBA MICHAEL C. HARGROVE VS. BENJAMIN PIERCE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Lift Stay Of Litigation
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 15, 2014, Line 3: PLAINTIFF MICHAEL C. HARGROVE DBA MICHAEL C. HARGROVE CONSTRUCTION'S Motion To Lift Stay Of Litigation. Denied. As held by this Court in February 2012, the parties agreed to contractual arbitration and all of Plaintiff's claims are subject to that arbitration agreement. The Court declines to essentially vacate that Order and reassert jurisdiction over this matter. California has a "strong public policy favoring contractual arbitration." (MKJA Inc. v. Fit Franchising LLC (2011) 191 Cal.App.4th 643, 661.) If the parties are unable to agree on a new arbitrator, either party may file a motion for the Court to appoint one, pursuant to CCP sec. 1281.6. The parties are ordered to complete the arbitration within nine months of entry of this Order. (See Bosworth v. Whitmore (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 536, 550, holding that the Court may set a completion date.) Plaintiff's request for attorney's fees, raised for the first time in reply, is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA VS. SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT,INC., A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 15, 2014, Line 4: DEFENDANT SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT,INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. The motion is denied. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether the duct that Mr. Smoot identified contained four electrical lines and whether defendant struck one of those lines. (Gilliand dec., par. 21; Smoot dec., pars. 5, 7, 9). The court overrules defendant's objection to the Smoot declaration. D'Amico v. Bd. of Medical Examiners (1974) 11 Cal.3d 1, 21, does not apply because Mr. Smoot's deposition testimony and declaration are not contradictory. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: L&A WONG FAMILY PROPERTY ASSOCIATION LLC, et al VS. HAROLD WONG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Further Responses To Req For Production Of Docs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, July 15, 2014, Line 2, PLAINTIFFs L&A WONG FAMILY PROPERTY ASSOCIATION LLC,, LEM WONG's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents (Part 1 of 2 of tentative ruling) Pro Tem Judge Conor Moore, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff failed to file a separate statement of facts along with the moving papers, as required by the CCP. Plaintiff's moving papers do not otherwise provide Defendant with the exact nature of the relief requested in the motion to compel. Plaintiff filed the separate statement along with the reply brief, but this was of no help to Defendant. Defendant was, therefore, unable to adequately oppose the Plaintiff's motion. This alone could serve as the basis for denying Plaintiff's motion. Plaintiff also does not appear to have adequately met and conferred with Defendant before filing this motion, which also may have allowed Defendant to better understand the motion and the grounds for opposition. In light of the regular and frequent contact between the parties, Plaintiff should have done more to alert Defendant to its intention to file the motion to compel to allow for informal resolution between the parties and to allow Defendant to better understand the dispute. Defendant's argument that the motion is time-barred is not well taken in light of Defendant's inability to demonstrate that the responses were verified until May 5, 2014. This, along with the rolling nature of the production, could have reasonably led Plaintiff to believe that Defendant had not fully completed its responses until that date. Even after the Court reviewed the separate statement of facts, it is not clear what relief Plaintiff is seeking. It appears that Defendant has produced all information responsive to Plaintiff's requests and has submitted a verified response. Accordingly, Plaintiff's motion to compel is denied and Plaintiff's request for sanctions against Defendant is denied. Plaintiff did not adequately meet and confer with Defendant, did not follow the CCP's requirements when filing the motion, and the moving papers do not provide substantial justification for Plaintiff's motion. Plaintiff and Plaintiff's counsel are therefore jointly and severally sanctioned in the amount of $1,000 total to allow Defendant can recover costs in opposing Plaintiff's motion (Code of Civ. Proc. 2031.310(h).(end of Part 1 of 2 of tentative ruling) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JUSTIN MOORE VS. RICHARD BURDEN TEED
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Protective Order And Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, July 15, 2014, Line 3, DEFENDANTs RICHARD TEED, TEED HAZE DEVELOPMENT, LLC's Motion For Protective Order And Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Conor Moore, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's requests for judicial notice are granted. Defendant is a party to the litigation and has filed a cross-complaint, and the Code of Civil Procedure requires that he sit for his deposition if properly noticed by the opposing party. CCP § 2025.010. Defendant has the burden of showing good cause why his deposition should be postponed. There appear to be two primary reasons Defendant has to delay the deposition: (1) awaiting an insurance coverage determination; and (2) awaiting the status of Cross-Defendant Donald Moore. Defendant's insurance company has verbally agreed to provide coverage (Reply, 3:21-22), so there does not appear to be any further reason to delay the deposition on those grounds. Cross-Defendant has filed a demurrer to the FAC, which if sustained will moot Defendant's arguments, and if it is not sustained, Cross-Defendant has the right to depose Defendant to prepare his defense to the cross-complaint on the issues specific to him. There does not appear to be any further reason to delay the deposition on this basis either. Accordingly, Defendant's motion for a protective order is denied. Defendant's request for sanctions against Defendant is denied. The Court has previously ordered Defendant's deposition for July 17, 2014. Defendant now indicates that he is unavailable for this date and counsel was not able to confer with Defendant prior to the Court ordering the deposition date. The deposition of Defendant is hereby continued to a mutually agreeable time, but in no event shall it begin later than July 31, 2014. Although Defendant's motion is denied, there does not appear to be an abuse of the discovery process, so Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sfjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. DOES 1-50, INCLUSIVE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 15, 2014, Line 9: DEFENDANTS KEVIN SULLIVAN and GRIFFIN & SULLIVAN, ATTORNEYS AT LAW'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons. The motion is granted. Plaintiff has the burden of demonstrating jurisdiction, including proper service. See Ziller Electronics Lab GmbH v. Sup. Crt. (1988) 206 Cal.App.3d 1222, 1232. Plaintiff fails to show that he effectuated personal service. The evidence indicates that Ms. Monge was not defendants' agent for the service of process.
